topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle, yellowed paper felt alien in my hands, the ink faded to a ghostly smudge. It was tucked inside a forgotten book, a relic from a time before my parents had settled into their comfortable routine. My fingers traced the elegant, looping script of my father’s signature, and a coldness began to spread through my chest, tightening my breath until it caught in my throat. Reading the words—a formal notice of departure, dated from twenty years prior—was like watching a carefully constructed wall crumble. I dropped the letter onto the dusty floorboards, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet room. I stood there, rigid, my knuckles white where I clenched my fists, the air suddenly too thin to draw in. A profound sense of disconnect washed over me, as if a vital piece of my understanding of them had simply vanished.

[story 2]

I found the envelope tucked behind the loose wallpaper in the attic. The paper was thin, almost transparent, and the handwriting was familiar, yet strangely distant. When I unfolded it, the contents spilled out: a resignation letter. My stomach clenched, a tight, nauseating knot forming just beneath my ribs. I read the formal language, the detached professionalism of the document, and a wave of profound disorientation hit me. I sank onto an old trunk, the wood groaning under my weight. My gaze kept flicking back to the script, my jaw working silently as I tried to reconcile this past figure with the person I thought I knew. A tremor ran through my hands, and I pressed my palms hard against my eyes, needing to physically block out the stark reality of what those words represented.

[story 3]

It was a mundane afternoon, sorting through boxes in the garage, when I unearthed the folded document. The paper was fragile, smelling faintly of age and stored dust. My heart began to beat a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. As I scanned the text—my mother’s signature anchoring the document—a sudden, sharp ache bloomed behind my eyes. I reread the lines, the formal declaration of leaving, and a strange, hollow sensation settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I let the letter slip from my grasp; it fluttered down onto the concrete floor. I didn't move to pick it up. Instead, I stood perfectly still, breathing shallowly, the silence of the garage amplifying the vast, unbridgeable distance that suddenly felt between us.
